all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for reporting the issue. the crash is caused by invalid inputs passed to [inline code] , rather than a problem in the split gpu kernel itself. i noticed two issues in your code: - axis is created using [inline code] , which produces a scalar tensor with value 1. for split, the axis must be a python integer or a scalar tensor containing the intended axis value. example of valid usage: [code block] - the input tensor has shape [73271, 29308], which is extremely large and requires about 8.6 gb of gpu memory. split then produces two outputs plus additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement exceeds 15 gb, which is beyond typical gpu limits. because of these invalid inputs, the gpu kernel receives incorrect offsets and attempts to read outside the allocated buffer, resulting in the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when valid axis values and reasonable tensor sizes are used, the op works correctly. i am attaching a [gist]( [url] for your reference. i hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

clean_title: localization process isn't aligned with weekly releases
clean_body: due to the process change of weekly releases, the process for localization of vs code is broken. a lot of it heavily relied on monthly branching: [url] even in our previous process monthly release process, the branching was done by endgame champ. additionally, the pipeline relies on building vs code in order to extract the strings from it... with vs code moving faster, keeping those build steps stable will be difficult and has already given me headaches over the years. with weekly releases this has to be rethought. ideas: can we not do this branch swapping and run the pipeline off of either the current release branch or even main? (this will need to make sure that the engine version is properly reflected in the resulting language packs) can we avoid building vscode in a satellite repo and instead bring this creation of english strings into core and then pass them to a pipeline in vscode-loc-drop? since we are in a broken state today, this is quite important to address as it means no new strings are translated.

(this will need to make sure that the engine version is properly reflected in the resulting language packs) can we avoid building vscode in a satellite repo and instead bring this creation of english strings into core and then pass them to a pipeline in vscode-loc-drop? since we are in a broken state today, this is quite important to address as it means no new strings are translated.
all_comments_text: sorry, this might have gotten lost in the slimmer endgame plan. tho, it is something we can easily add back but also any process simplification and cleanup is very welcomed |SEP| i've always had trouble keeping the entire localization machinery in my head. so much so that i never really knew how it fully works. please feel free to take the opportunity to dramatically simplify it! inlining as much as possible to the core product build sounds like a great first step. |SEP| ok so, given the importance of figuring this out quickly due to not getting new strings, i've made a short fix that doesn't make things worse and simplifies things a bit: we now run [this pipeline]( [url] daily, which will check for the latest [inline code] branch, if there's a newer one, it will do what it did before and update the repo to track that new branch. this will bump the engine version so everyone's happy. this means that endgame champ no longer has to run a pipeline to do this. one less step ✅ this means that new strings will only go in when we branch, and with an sla of 3 days, they may be slightly delayed before the release... but that's not too bad. i think it's ok. plus, weekly releases, if a string doesn't make it, oh well, wait a week, it'll be there no problem. not the end of the world. possible improvement of that: release language packs when they change instead of on a schedule. that'll really be reactive... it's probably ok to do this, i think. ✅ [url] we still keep the same "[mirroring of vs code build steps]( [url] which isn't great but it's also what we had before so that's ok tracked: [url] |SEP| moving to 1.113 for verification to give localization some extra time to propagate the new strings.

clean_title: subagent github rate-limit errors do not surface to the parent agent and do not show the "try again" action
clean_body: type: bug does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: n/a (copilot agent/subagent workflow) vs code version: unknown os version: macos steps to reproduce: 1. start a top-level copilot agent task that invokes a subagent. 2. have the subagent hit a github-side rate limit/error. 3. observe the parent/top-level conversation. expected: - the subagent failure should surface in the top-level conversation. - the same retry affordance (for example a "try again" button) should be available from the top-level context. actual: - the top-level conversation does not surface the rate-limit error. - no "try again" affordance appears at the top level. - the only way to see the failure is to open the subagent history and scroll to the bottom. additional notes: - a screenshot is available showing the nested subagent history error state. - this appears to be specifically about error propagation / retry ux from subagent - parent conversation, not just the existence of rate limiting itself.

clean_title: [inductor][cuda][test] test mm plus mm3 gpu wrapper fails with xpass on cuda.
clean_body: the test [inline code] in [inline code] fails with [xpass(strict)] on cuda because of the xfail. test class : inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py::testgpuwrapper::test mm plus mm3 gpu wrapper platform : cuda error : xpass(strict) - test was expected to fail but passes steps to reproduce 1. build pytorch with cuda support. 2. run: [inline code] 4. observe [inline code] failing with [xpass(strict)] because it passes but is marked as expected to fail. root cause pr [ ]( [url] added an xfail on [inline code] in [inline code] with the reason "c++ wrapper dynamic shapes fails on cuda, fixed on rocm." on cuda, the test [inline code] actually passes, so it fails with [inline code] error. only the dynamic shapes variant [inline code] fails on cuda. so the xfail is too broad: it marks both tests as expected to fail when only the dynamic shapes run fails. proposed fix: remove the xfail from [inline code] and skip the dynamic shapes variant on cuda in [inline code] (similar to test mm plus mm2 dynamic shapes). so that the [inline code] test runs and passes on cuda. the dynamic shapes test is skipped on cuda (where it fails) and runs on rocm (where it passes). hence, for [inline code] , if we remove the xfail on the test and use a skip in [inline code] the dynamic variant which is known to fail on cuda is skipped and the xpass issue will be resolved. i have a fix for this and if this is a good approach i can go ahead with this. code references base test : test/inductor/test select algorithm.py:299 (test mm plus mm3) gpu wrapper config : test/inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py:107-125 (test failures gpu wrapper) cc: -xq -turnansky cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: extension update fails silently - no error message shown
clean_body: issue description when attempting to update an extension from the extensions panel, the update process fails without showing any error message to the user. screenshot as shown in the screenshot, the claude code for vs code extension displays an "update" button, but clicking it results in a silent failure with no error feedback. steps to reproduce 1. open vs code 2. navigate to the extensions panel 3. find an extension with an available update (showing "update" button) 4. click the "update" button 5. the update appears to start but then fails silently expected behavior - the extension should update successfully, or - if the update fails, a clear error message should be displayed explaining why actual behavior - the update button is clicked - no visual feedback or error message is shown - the extension remains at the old version - the "update" button continues to be displayed environment - vs code version: latest stable - os: macos (based on screenshot ui) - extension: claude code for vs code (anthropic) additional context the extensions panel shows the update is available, but clicking the update button does not complete the update process. this appears to be a general issue with the extension update mechanism rather than specific to any particular extension. suggestions 1. add proper error handling and user feedback when extension updates fail 2. show a notification or inline error message explaining the failure reason 3. provide retry options or troubleshooting steps

clean_title: xla jit compilation fails with string tensor return values
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf 2.21.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution no response mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? when using [inline code] to enable xla jit compilation, any function that returns a string tensor fails to compile. the error occurs because xla's return value mechanism ( [inline code] operation) does not support string data types ( [inline code] ). the code works correctly without jit compile=true, but fails when xla compilation is enabled. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
clean_text: xla jit compilation fails with string tensor return values issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf 2.21.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution no response mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? when using [inline code] to enable xla jit compilation, any function that returns a string tensor fails to compile. the error occurs because xla's return value mechanism ( [inline code] operation) does not support string data types ( [inline code] ). the code works correctly without jit compile=true, but fails when xla compilation is enabled. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce the same issue using tensorflow versions 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. i am providing a [gist]( [url] here for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi , thanks for your patience. this is expected. xla only supports numerical tensor types, so [inline code] tensors are not supported as inputs or return values when [inline code] is enabled. all operations inside a jit-compiled function must be xla-compatible, so returning a string tensor leads to a compilation error. i have attached a colab [gist]( [url] showing a minimal example. i hope this helps clarify the behavior. thank you!

clean_title: avoid cuda sync in spectral decompositions
clean_body: 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch current docs of spectral decompositions say that they require a device < -- cpu sync. i don't know if it's because of the sync, but in practice i am able to compute spectral decompositions for a batch of matrices using cupy interop via dlpack approximatley 10 times faster than with pytorch - and this includes a custom autograd function that also implements backprop for me (500x100x100 tensor, of 500 matrices of size 100x100). i am assuming that if cupy does it quickly, then nvidia already provided some primitives to do it quickly. so i am asking, if it's possible, to use them in pytorch internally as well. alternatives continue using cupy/dlpack interop. additional context i am working on researching trainable models whose nonlinear activations are matrix eigenvalues. making it fast will significantly accelerate my experiments without any special cupy hacks. cc

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here. i checked your code, and the main cause appears to be the extremely large tensor shape you are using. as observed, the tensor size allocates over 8 gb of gpu memory, which can easily exhaust or fragment available gpu resources. when gpu memory is exhausted or fragmented, compute-sanitizer may incorrectly report this as an “invalid read.” i verified this on my side with smaller tensors using tensorflow versions 2.20.0 and 2.19.0, and it worked fine without any errors. i have also attached a colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. please try using smaller tensor shapes to confirm that the issue is related to memory size rather than a kernel bug. hope this helps! thank you.
solution_comments: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here. i checked your code, and the main cause appears to be the extremely large tensor shape you are using. as observed, the tensor size allocates over 8 gb of gpu memory, which can easily exhaust or fragment available gpu resources. when gpu memory is exhausted or fragmented, compute-sanitizer may incorrectly report this as an “invalid read.” i verified this on my side with smaller tensors using tensorflow versions 2.20.0 and 2.19.0, and it worked fine without any errors. i have also attached a colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. please try using smaller tensor shapes to confirm that the issue is related to memory size rather than a kernel bug. hope this helps! thank you.

all_comments_text: what’s broken is that torch.compile (torchdynamo + inductor) miscompiles dtype-changing view() operations by treating them as safe aliasing views, even though they reinterpret storage at the byte level. which is why compiling an unrelated bf16 path or adding a “no-op” reshape changes the q6 k result (the compiled kernel ends up reading misaligned or stale bytes, yielding 0.0625 instead of 1.0) the proper fix in pytorch is to treat dtype-changing views as unsafe, either force a materializing copy (or alignment/lifetime barrier) when view(dtype) changes element size, or tighten alias analysis so inductor cannot reuse storage across such reinterpret casts. a practical workaround is to avoid view(dtype) under torch.compile: replace it with to(dtype) (copy), or insert clone() / contiguous() right after any dtype reinterpretation, or keep gguf dequant code out of compiled regions; all of these prevent buffer reuse and restore correctness at the cost of a small copy. |SEP| does this diagnosis and proposed fix sound correct to you? should [inline code] that changes element size be treated as unsafe in inductor (copy-on-view or a no-alias/lifetime barrier), rather than a normal aliasing view, to prevent cross-graph buffer reuse and miscompilation in cases like gguf dequant? |SEP| [inline code] (like [inline code] in c++) is different from [inline code] (like [inline code] ), so it needs to be separately implemented in [inline code] . let's see how the pytorch developers plan to implement this. currently i'm working around this using explicit bit manipulation: [url] |SEP| this seems like inductor issue i have tried with aot eager inductor decomps backend and the accuracy passes, can you take a look? this is high prio, not just because of incorrect results, but also because it affects comfyui which relies on torch.compile to speedup image-video gen models. |SEP| i did root cause and added debug on above repro code. my observation is that some shapes in compile of qk 6 while we are using factory wrapper like (wrap compile) are created as dynamic shape. as compile think it is an extension of earlier compile. if i do add dynamo reset in between 2 compiles the code works as expected. i think this issue can be seen anywhere if someone is using factory wrapper but different closures. the fix is to add hashed closures as property in class codeid so that whenever we use different closures we generate different codeid. and , do let me know your opinion on the root cause and fix. |SEP| yes it happens only when a closure is used. if i remove [inline code] and directly call [inline code] , it does not happen. |SEP| cc , i tested this only reprod with dynamic shapes. thus the recompile. can you take a look ? |SEP| added this to the bug bash to look at next week |SEP| removing myself from assignees. i think i might have been added by mistake. please feel free to re-assign me if my attention is needed. |SEP| torchdynamo generates identical cache keys (codeid) for factory-wrapped functions (like wrap compile) because it hashes the code object without hashing the bound closure variables, causing the second compilation (dequantize blocks q6 k) to falsely hit the cache of the first (dequantize blocks bf16) and execute with stale static assumptions (e.g. wrong shape/stride constants for view(dtype)). [code block] |SEP| -logic , attached pr does similar work. can you review the attached pr? |SEP| -in code review & audit: pr first off, excellent root-cause analysis. you successfully traced a very stealthy cache-poisoning bug back to its origin: dynamo treating dynamically generated functions (closures) as identical because it was only hashing the code object. the added test case (test factory created functions separate pgo state) is bulletproof and perfectly isolates the issue. however, while the fix works for this specific reproducer, there are a couple of system-level edge cases regarding python's cell semantics and distributed caching that we need to harden before this gets merged into main. here is my audit: 1. the valueerror blast radius (cell empty exception) in python, accessing c.cell contents on an uninitialized cell doesn't return none; it raises valueerror: cell is empty. in your implementation: [code block] because the try...except wraps the entire tuple comprehension, if even one cell in the closure is uninitialized, the exception is thrown, the comprehension aborts, and closure hash silently falls back to none. this leaves the function vulnerable to the exact same cache collision bug we are trying to fix if it happens to have mixed initialized/uninitialized cells. fix: handle the valueerror on a per-cell basis. 2. the ephemerality of id() and remote pgo in get closure content: [code block] using id(content) is a classic trap in caching layers. id() is tied to the memory address of the object, which is strictly tied to the lifecycle of the current python process. if a user is leveraging remote pgo (which relies on codeid to serialize state across distributed nodes or disk caches—see automatic dynamic remote pgo in pgo.py), id() will result in non-deterministic hashes across different runs. this will cause remote cache misses and trigger silent recompilations. fix: if the object isn't a code object, attempt to hash its actual value (if it is immutable/hashable) before falling back to id(). we want to preserve determinism as much as possible for remote caches. suggested code refactoring here is a hardened version of your logic that handles both the per-cell valueerror and improves the determinism of the hash: [code block] summary the pr is fundamentally correct in its approach. update the valueerror handling to prevent the whole hash generation from aborting. patch id() to prioritize actual object hashing for remote cache determinism. drop these updates in, and from an engineering perspective, this is a beautiful fix to a nasty bug. great job tracking this down!

clean_title: [numerical consistency] significant drift between eager and torch.compile triggered by inductor shape padding=1 on conv2d(1x1)
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i observed a significant numerical discrepancy ($ 10^{-3}$ max absolute error) between pytorch eager mode and torch.compile when testing a standard 1x1 convolution. the issue is specifically triggered or amplified when the optimization flag inductor shape padding=1 is enabled. why this matters: this level of error ($10^{-3}$) is unacceptable for a single layer in a deep network. in architectures like mobilenet or resnet, such numerical drift in early layers can accumulate and propagate through dozens of subsequent layers, eventually leading to inconsistent top-1 classification results or training divergence. the promise of torch.compile is bit-wise or near bit-wise consistency, which is broken here. all_comments_text: [physics reject] the $10^{-3}$ discrepancy is working as intended; enabling inductor shape padding=1 forces memory alignment for tensor core utilization, inherently triggering implicit tensorfloat-32 (tf32) low-precision matrix kernels on ampere gpus, meaning the observed error is strictly bounded by the mathematical limits of the tf32 10-bit mantissa. |SEP| -logic for users who prioritize consistency over peak throughput, please provide an implementation that avoids inf - inf (nan) scenarios in fused kernels, ensuring predictable behavior even under extreme values. 2. consistent error exposure: our core request is to ensure that numerical anomalies are exposed in a consistent form across different backends, rather than manifesting as completely different error states (e.g., nan on gpu vs. masked "valid" values on cpu). |SEP| bitwise equivalence is not yet expected here. closing. |SEP| hi -logic i understand that bitwise equivalence is not a current goal, but there is a significant usability issue here. the core problem is that inductor shape padding is marketed as a memory alignment optimization, yet it implicitly toggles precision-altering hardware paths (tf32) without the user's explicit consent. if a user has already set torch.set float32 matmul precision('highest'), they expect fp32 precision. if shape padding=1 overrides this global precision setting to enable tensor cores, it violates the principle of least astonishment. at the very least, could this side-effect be documented, or should inductor respect the global matmul precision setting when deciding whether to pad for tensor cores? closing this as "intended" ignores the fact that users are losing 13 bits of precision silently.

clean_title: bug report: vs code macos - "end of central directory record signature not found"
clean_body: does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes - vs code version: last 3 versions (tested on all, same issue) - os version: macos productname: macos productversion: 15.7.3 buildversion: 24g419 steps to reproduce: 1. open vs code (any of the last 3 versions). 2. wait for the extension host and cloud sync initialization. 3. observe in the log the error: text error: end of central directory record signature not found. either not a zip file, or file is truncated. at n0 (...) at /node modules/yauzl/index.js:40:7 ... expected behavior: vs code should start normally without zip/extraction errors. actual behavior: vs code logs the error about “end of central directory record signature not found” and some features (like settings sync) fail to initialize properly. error seems related to the internal zip extraction of extensions or cloud sync data. additional notes: happens even when all extensions are disabled (code --disable-extensions). the error appears in the temporary apptranslocation folder on macos, which may indicate an issue with macos gatekeeper/app translocation. the problem occurs consistently across the last 3 vs code releases.

clean_body: i observed that in pytorch 2.10, the enter and exit methods of streamcontextvariablein the dynamo component have removed the logic for creating proxies via tx.output.create proxy. in contrast, pytorch 2.9 retains this logic: [code block] this change causes the set stream nodes to disappear from fx graphs in pytorch 2.10+, whereas they were present in 2.9. related pr maybe: [url] example [code block] in torch2.9, fx graph will be: [code block] impact:​ my custom torch.compilebackend relies on these set stream nodes in the fx graph to perform stream-related operations before aotautograd. the removal of these nodes breaks my workflow, as the necessary stream configuration is no longer captured in the graph. questions: what motivated the removal of create proxyin streamcontextvariable? are there alternative approaches to retain the original behavior (e.g., explicit stream management) in newer dynamo versions? cc -chen -nrv

all_comments_text: "i have performed a step-by-step comparison of these execution modes using a single-variable control approach (specifically comparing cuda/fp32/0000 vs. cuda/fp16/0000 and cuda/fp32/0000 vs. cuda/fp32/0010). therefore, i believe the issue originates from inductor. the results are as follows:" [code block] [code block] as shown in the results above, even fp32 + compile only exhibits an error of 3.28e-04, exceeding the 1e-4 threshold by 3.3x. although per-layer errors appear modest, in deep networks like densenet, errors accumulate at each layer, causing the final output error to grow exponentially and ultimately change the predicted labels. i have verified these label changes, and evidence is available upon request. |SEP| , did you try same with latest pytorch version? i am not seeing this in latest pytorch version. |SEP| -in thanks for checking! i can confirm that i'm still observing the same numerical discrepancy issue on the latest pytorch version: 2.10.0+cu126, with cuda 12.6 and an nvidia rtx 3090 gpu. [code block] |SEP| , i tried with wt files shared earlier in repro and nn.conv2d(3, 96, kernel size=3, stride=2, padding=3) as weights saved were of this order. i get following output: [code block] fp32-eager /fp-32 compile is showing minimal diff and others are expected. if you can share new model wts. then i can try the same on my system. also note i am using cuda version 12.8 |SEP| [physics reject] the $3.28 \times 10^{-4}$ discrepancy under inductor shape padding=1 is strictly working as intended; enabling compiler-level shape padding aligns the spatial memory pitch to satisfy ampere architecture tensor core requirements, inherently forcing the underlying heuristic dispatchers to silently abandon exact fp32 simd instructions in favor of highly optimized tensorfloat-32 (tf32) matrix multiplication algorithms, meaning the observed drift is purely a hardware-level truncation artifact bounded by the tf32 10-bit mantissa, not a triton code logic bug. |SEP| i tested [inline code] vs. [inline code] and believe it's not the cause of the numerical difference. [inline code] is set to true by default, and then when conv2d is computed in tf32 mode, due to it having fewer mantissa bits than fp32, the numerical difference will accumulate, causing the error. you can try to set [inline code] and run fp32 compile vs. fp32 eager comparison again, and i believe they should match.

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] in [inline code] does: [code block] reserve(1) allocates capacity but keeps size() at 0. accessing [0] is undefined behavior. with libc++ hardening ( libcpp hardening mode extensive), operator[] includes bounds checking and calls builtin trap() on violation, producing sigtrap at runtime. fix: reserve(1) → resize(1). the same pattern existed in aten/src/aten/native/mps/operations/shape.mm (inputtensors .reserve) but was incidentally eliminated by the refactor in . versions found by code inspection. present in current main (as of a79cc95) and all releases through v2.10.0. cc -arm

clean_title: improve screen reader labels: expose implicit context, include disable-setting hint, and label remove button
clean_body: accessibility problem some ui context is currently implicit (visual-only) and is not obvious to screen reader users. the related controls should communicate this context directly in accessible names. requested improvements 1. make the implicit context explicit in the element aria-label so screen reader users get the same meaning that sighted users infer visually. 2. include the relevant setting in the aria-label/help text so users know how to disable this behavior from settings. 3. add a clear aria-label for the remove button. why this matters when context is only conveyed visually, screen reader users can miss key meaning and available actions. including the disable-setting hint in the accessible text also improves discoverability and control. repro/observation - in the ui shown in the attached screenshot, key context appears to be implicit. - screen reader output does not sufficiently expose that context. - the remove control should have an explicit and descriptive aria-label. expected behavior - aria-label includes the missing context. - aria-label/help exposes which setting disables this behavior. - remove button has a descriptive aria-label (for example, what is being removed). notes - this request is specifically about improving accessibility semantics and discoverability for screen reader users. - screenshot referenced from report context.
